data.title: BlackBerry: Innovation Is Fueling Profitability Amid Short-Term Headwinds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my previous article, I highlighted the growth prospects from the QNX software, with over 24 top electric vehicles out of 25 using QNX software, positioning the company for a 10-year growth. I also mentioned that in 24 out of 25 electric vehicles, manufacturers are using the QNX software. This was my idea in my previous bullish angle on the company bouncing back.

In this article, I am rating BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) as a Hold based on the company’s innovation through the angle of large portions of R&D investments on developing software products and services for the QNX and Secure Communications solutions. However, I treat the current bearish trend of BlackBerry as a result of short-term market challenges in the automotive sector. The global automotive industry is experiencing an economic downturn due to high inflation and costly raw materials, impacting consumer purchasing power and cost of production and profits.

As a result, the company has also been reducing its guidance to make more realistic projections. In fiscal year 2024, the company reduced its R&D by 10.1%, equivalent to $21 million compared to fiscal year 2023. This led to a Q4 total revenue outlook of between $126 million and $135 million, which is ~20% down compared to Q3 2025 total revenue of $162 million. In the fiscal year 2025, the R%D reduced by $18.3 million, equivalent to 14.4% compared to fiscal year 2024. The company beat its Q4 2025 total revenue guidance range by $65.8 million, which means it is meeting its projections amid current market headwinds. In my holding thesis, I want you to recall that the company just hit positive free cash flow, and Q4 2025 saw $41.5 million from a negative FCF in Q4 2024 of $16.3 million Year-over-Year ((YoY)).

Below, I explain further why I think BlackBerry is a Hold, which is a downgrade in the short-term, as I look forward to the automotive sector getting some relief from inflation, increasing cost of production, reducing consumer purchasing power, and reducing automotive manufacturers profits.

Innovation Aspect In QNX Context

As of Q4 2025, the IoT segment, which has been renamed QNX, will be integrated into over 255 million automobiles in 2025, plus other industry utilities such as robotics, medical devices, industrial controls, and heavy machinery. This level of achievement is accompanied by high investment in R&D at an average of ~20.8% of the last three fiscal year’s revenues, after general and administrative expenses averaging 29.1% of the same fiscal year revenues. This confirms why the company’s QNX is the current leader in embedded software. From this investment, the company maintains its customers, such as leading OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers, using its software in their automobiles.

So far, the company has achieved working with over 10 OEMs, 24 out of 25 leading EV OEMs, 7 Tier 1 suppliers, and 9 out of 10 leading medical OEMs. To further build on my previous article, why I think BlackBerry is on its bouncing back phase is when I look at the Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV) demand and its preparedness through maintaining the lead in automotive software. The SDVs are making critical mechanical functions like braking, steering, and infotainment more effective and efficient while managed by software with high levels of safety and an update option. BlackBerry has a high upside chance due to the SDVs market, which is projected to hit 7.6 million units by the end of 2025 from 2024 6.2 million units.

From this thought process, I am connecting it with BlackBerry’s current opportunity to work with 15 out of 20 leading global Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) solutions. This is having access to 80% of the global light passenger car volume at a time when the automotive industry is moving from Level 2 autonomy to Level 2.5 and 3. Remember, BlackBerry’s QNX has achieved the L3 qualification threshold, an edge ahead of its competitors. For example, safety is one of the concerns in L3, which its ONX has achieved.

I am focused on the company’s innovation because the company is committed to taking a bigger share of 43% of the market share in North America with continuous innovation that meets new-age OEMs. However, I recommend holding right now because the bearish trend will not last long with the current modest projected growth in 2025 at 91.4 million light vehicle units, a 3.1% increase from 2024. The automotive sector is recovering slowly, which ideally means cautious optimism. The company is also cautiously optimistic, pending the automotive industry recovery, which I think will be short-term.

Short-Term Headwinds

I mentioned in my thesis statement that BlackBerry is facing a slow automotive market recovery headwind, but its performance is pegged on its innovation. I believe these are short-term headwinds because the Q4 2025 release shows that the company is surpassing its guidance. For example, the total revenue in Q4 2025 surpassed the expected total revenue of $130 million in Q3 2025 to $141.7 million. Secure communication recorded the larger share of revenues. Still, my focus on QNX is because this segment has an opportunity yet to be fully utilized with the current innovation at Level 3 success. Also, looking at the lower cost of sales ((CoS)) used in the segment in Q4 2025, the QNX segment recorded a dismal decline from $65.9 million for $9.7 million on Feb 29, 2024, to $65.8 million at the cost of $11.1 million in Feb 28 2025 at a change of increased CoS by 1.4 YoY. Despite recording more revenues, secure communication has a high CoS of YoY increase of 5.6.

From this explanation above, you can now understand that despite the cautious outlook revealed by the company's declining revenue guidance, the company surpassed the guidance, especially from QNX, with minimal CoS. I attribute BlackBerry surpassing its revenue guidance to innovation success in the segment. My reasoning in bringing up QNX’s success is based on the company's QNX segment scoring 84% of adjusted gross margin, compared to secure communication at 66% gross margin. At the same time, it recorded a higher revenue in Q4 2025. The QNX gains while the automotive industry has a slow recovery shows that the company's potential from its R&D investments is in software solutions. In 2025, global auto sales are expected to hit 89.6 million, a rise of 1.7% YoY, reflecting a cautious recovery in growth.

Electric Vehicles (EVs), which are also expected to record 15.1 million units in 2025, an increase of 30% compared to 2024, is a sector where BlackBerry also supplies software solutions. 2025 vehicle production outlook is modest, showing a stagnation with more global risk continuing to intensify, such as U.S. tariffs.

In my opinion, The current bearish move is short-term because the governments are navigating a balance between suppressing inflation and boosting growth.

Financials: Q4 2025 Highlights

The company's total revenue in Q4 2025 was $141.7 million, which surpassed quarter guidance by 8.46% from $130 million. The quarter also saw the full fiscal year FY 2025 surpassing the guidance range of $517 million and $526 million by recording a $534.9 million fiscal year. This delivered a 74% adjusted gross margin and a GAAP gross margin of 73%. This reveals that the company has strong financial health because of its efficient cost management. The company reduced salaries and benefits expenses by $9.4 million and decreased consulting costs by $7.1 million. The adjusted EBITDA exceeded the previous quarter’s guidance of $10 to $20 million by recording $21.1 million.

Operating cash flow surpassed expectations by $57 million YoY by recording $42 million. This cash flow improved total cash and investments by $144 million to $410 million. The net loss reduced from $130.2 million in Q4 2024 to $79.0 million in Q4 2025.

Overall, the company has a healthy financial performance, which is a reason to wait for the automotive industry to stabilize.

Valuation

In my previous article, my bullish rating was also supported by the company's good performance, such as the Price-to-Sales ratio (P/S ratio) undervaluation score of 4.856. In this article, I pick the same competitors as previously: Cloudflare, Inc (NYSE:NET), Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (NASDAQ:PANW), and Cerene Inc. (NASDAQ:CRNC), to evaluate BlackBerry's position in valuation metrics. The P/S ratio shows the following:

The P/S ratio metric shows BB is still undervalued based on the level of business it has with the leading OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers. With about 255 million cars operating BlackBerry software and more automobile manufacturers seeking its services, the company is still undervalued against its competitors. The high P/S ratio of Cloudflare, Inc. at 21.96 is attributed to the company's strong demand for its cybersecurity solutions in cloud adoption and AI-driven automation.

The EV/EBITDA:

BB's EV/EBITDA ratio was unavailable in the previous article. In this article, the company recorded 27.86 after PANW at 78.43 due to its high-security solutions for its apps and devices. The company also has precision AI that detects and remediates problems. The P/S ratio of NET is high due to its booming cybersecurity business, but its EV/EBITDA is very low at negative 1,016.77. It shows that the company's current price of $107.39 is overvalued against its EBITDA, which does not reveal the price performance according to actual business performance in my opinion.

Going to cash from operations:

Overall, PANW is the ideal buy investment with cash from operations of $3.11 billion compared to close competitor NET at $380.43 million. The company is not overvalued at 78.43 EV/EBITDA and 12.56 P/S ratio. I recommend a hold for BlackBerry because it just hit positive cash from operations, currently at $16.50 million, meaning that the company is on an upside trajectory but has been halted by market headwinds. At 27.87 EV/EBITDA and 3.47 P/S ratio, BlackBerry has potential for growth because it shows that at the level of success it has made, as discussed above, the company is positioned to benefit from the automobile industry.

Investment Risks

  • Global economic risks – The company depends largely on consumers' high purchasing power. When the purchasing power is dwindling, the sales plummet, and the company profits when the consumer purchasing power is high. The current inflation has reduced consumer purchasing power, which is not certain when it will end with U.S. proposed tariffs.
  • Competition – The company reduced its financial outlook because the automobile market's slow growth is reducing its investment in R&D. This sparks risks of being overtaken by competitors.
  • Dependence on Key customers - In the case of unprecedented occurrences where these customers reduce their business with the company, the company will likely experience a substantial impact on already declining revenues. However, additional customers for its Level 3 software solutions could also help its revenues recover significantly.

data.title: Keep Calm And Carry On: Top 5 AI Stocks For April 2025
data.description:
Despite Growth, AI Stocks Dip Amid Global Tariff Turmoil

Tariff-induced turmoil continued to grip the market on Tuesday after a brief rally by U.S stocks—pushed higher on hopes that the U.S. would strike trade deals to lower Donald Trump’s aggressive tariffs—quickly fizzled out in afternoon trading. After nearing their best sessions since November 2022, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite shed most of their gains from earlier in the session.

The latest volatility follows three days of sweeping losses after the president signaled that he would not back down from tariffs despite pleas from billionaire backers and fears of a global recession. Trump is now threatening that new 50% tariffs will be imposed on China unless it cancels its own retaliatory levies on U.S. exports, while his administration signals that it is open to negotiations.

"Over those following few days from April 2 we had, I think we're up to 70 countries, contact the White House for how to come and negotiate," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told CNBC on Tuesday. The latest messaging from team Trump comes after Goldman Sachs has raised the odds of a recession from 35% to 45%, and Federal Reserve chair Jay Powell warned that the tariffs will spark “higher inflation and slower growth” if they continue to go on as planned.

“It is now becoming clear that the tariff increases will be significantly larger than expected,” Powell said during a conference in Virginia earlier this month. “The same is likely to be true of the economic effects, which will include higher inflation and slower growth.” Until more is known about the fallout from the tariffs, the U.S. central bank will likely keep its interest rates between 4.25% and 4.5%, despite a recent Truth Social post from Trump arguing that “This would be a PERFECT time for Fed Chairman Jerome Powell to cut Interest Rates.” Despite the negative news on the market and the economy, AI is still full steam ahead as can be seen on Seeking Alpha’s AI Tech Newswire with reports of increased capex spending, acquisitions, and capital commitments.

AI: a Bright Spot in Dark Times

As doom and gloom permeates Wall Street, there have been some bright spots in Silicon Valley as new artificial intelligence advancements power the industry forward. MSFT’s OpenAI raised $40bn in new funding from SoftBank and other investors earlier this month, securing a $300B valuation. The latest injection will “push the frontiers of AI research even further,” the ChatGPT maker stated, as it revealed it was building an “open weight” generative AI model in the face of fierce competition from China’s DeepSeek and domestic rivals like Meta (META), whose lower-cost models are freely available and can be adjusted by users.

Even as markets take a beating and DeepSeek continues to flex its low-cost, self-improving AI models, Silicon Valley’s AI spending spree shows little sign of slowing down. U.S. tech leaders plan to spend upwards of $320B on AI initiatives this year, up from $246B in 2024, as they race to expand sprawling data centers and invest heavily in specialized chips to power the latest innovations.

For some, those investments have already begun to pay off. Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) decision to combine its Gemini and DeepMind AI has helped propel 30.6% year-over-year growth from its cloud business in Q4 as AI features begin to popup across its suite of applications. As SA analyst Motti Sapir recently wrote:

This isn’t a trade for next week. It’s a long-term position for people who care about fundamentals, not headlines. Alphabet is turning AI from a research project into a revenue stream, and the market hasn’t caught up.

Sapir’s outlook could not resonate more as tariffs, sticky inflation and slowing economic growth stoke fears that the U.S. could be on the fast track to a ‘stagflation’ or a full-blown recession. Uncertainty is a headwind to growth, and as long as Trump’s economic agenda breeds chaos in the market, falling shares are inevitable. That’s why it is imperative to look beyond current volatility and invest for the long-term, prioritizing “fundamentals, not headlines.”

‘Fundamentals, Not Headlines’

Trump tariffs have taken markets on a roller coaster of volatility, and it’s understandable why investors might want to get off the ride altogether. But in times of economic uncertainty, panic selling often results in higher losses. In my latest webinar, Investing During a Correction, I discussed the importance of maintaining a disciplined investment process and removing emotion from the equation. Take it from me: fear fades and the market always returns to fundamentals.

You may be familiar with the old adage, “Fear creates losses.” However, intelligent investors can use fear to their favor. History shows us that it can often be true. Since 2010, the market has pulled back five times by more than 15% (July 1, 2010; August 8, 2011; December 20, 2018; March 9, 2020; and May 9, 2022). Investors who purchased the top 10 Quant Strong Buy stocks during these 15% downturns and held them for 2 years saw 117% gains on average vs. the S&P 500’s 50% return, according to an analysis of SA Quant data.

SA Quant Strong Buys Surged An Avg. 117% Following 15% Market Corrections

Here at Seeking Alpha Quant, we use the GARP strategy, aka Growth At A Reasonable Price. This approach can be especially helpful in a downmarket because it strips away fear and outside noise from the investment process. Using a combination of powerful computer processing and what I like to call our special ‘Quantamental’ analysis, our proprietary algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong ones based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. Using these insights, it ranks them in terms of valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions, assigning letter grades to each category to help contextualize thousands of data points.

If the proper fundamentals are there, our system can help detect stocks with the highest return-generating potential—often before the rest of the market—-because it doesn’t get distracted by falling shares or other emotion-inducing events. With that in mind, I’ve selected five top AI stocks for April, all of which have impressive growth rates, strong valuation frameworks, solid profitability, increasing upward EPS Revisions by analysts, and excellent momentum despite falling shares.

How I Picked the Top AI Stocks

To identify the top-performing stocks in the AI universe, I’ve used the Quant system to analyze securities from three of the largest AI ETFs—the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ), the Robo Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (ROBO), and the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ)—and selected five top-performing stocks to represent a diverse range of AI opportunities. They include tech companies that sell AI-powered solutions and non-tech firms that employ AI to boost productivity, improve products, and streamline operations. The five AI-leveraging stocks have returned an average of around 18.8% over the past 12 months vs. the S&P’s -0.66%. and are well-positioned to build upon that performance once the market begins to stabilize.

Top 5 AI Stocks for April, by Quant Rating (as of 4/8/25)

Let’s examine each stock in further detail.

1. Amazon, Inc. (NASDAQ: AMZN)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.86T

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Broadline Retail

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 14 out of 481

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 3 out of 27

Amazon (AMZN)—down more than 13% over the past month—has been no exception from the sell-off that has gripped global markets, and its deliveries from China will inevitably take a hit from Trump’s tariffs on China. However, the e-commerce giant’s mammoth size, strong profitability, and powerful growth-value proposition keep it high on my list.

Highlighted within my recent piece, Turmoil to Opportunity: Top 10 Quant Stocks for April, AMZN crushed Q4 revenue estimates by over $500M while beating earnings by $0.38. Its Amazon Web Services unit demonstrated impressive performance, with 48% Y/Y operating income growth and $39.8B in trailing operating income. The cloud business, which operates sprawling data centers, is well positioned to profit from global advancements in AI as U.S. tech superpowers like GOOG and OpenAI rise to meet the challenge posed by DeepSeek and increasing domestic competition.

Amazon Web Services Reports Strong Operational Performance in Q4

As SA analyst JR Research wrote on Monday:

Amazon's fundamentally strong business might help it to fend off the most debilitating effects on its supply chain even as smaller peers suffer. AWS is another highly profitable business that should underpin the tariff tirade, allowing Amazon to capitalize in its scale to gain market share as others reel from the damage.

AWS also recently unveiled a new quantum computing chip to compete with the likes of GOOG and MSFT, further boosting the company’s overall profitability and resilience in the fast-growing sector.

AMZN Stock Quant Breakdown

AMZN’s ‘B’-rated PEG (FWD) of 1.3 suggests it is evenly valued compared to its peers in the consumer discretionary sector—a rare occurrence for the notoriously overvalued company. Its forward P/E of 27 is an 87% premium to the sector, but that still looks compelling given AMZN’s multi-trillion-dollar market cap. Zooming out, the company’s overall valuation has improved from a ‘D-’ to a ‘D’ over the past six months, highlighting a window of opportunity that could close when markets improve.

The tech giant registers an ‘A-’ Growth Grade overall, pushed higher by forward EPS diluted growth of 38%, a 477% difference from the sector median. Enduring profitability is underscored by its massive 116B cash in operations and a 24.29% return on common equity.

Thanks to its low valuation, strong growth outlook powered by AI tailwinds, and powerful cloud business, it could be a great time to buy the dip on AMZN. Similar to the retail heavyweight, my next selection packs a punch despite its falling shares.

2. Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE: UBER)

  • Market Capitalization: $137.3B

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Passenger Transportation

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 15 out of 613

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 1 out of 8

Uber (UBER) is a top-performing Alpha Pick, having generated more than 70% to the portfolio since it was added in June, 2023. The rideshare and tech group received a major boost in February after Ackman’s Pershing Square disclosed a stake in the company, posting on X:

We believe that Uber is one of the best managed and highest quality businesses in the world. Remarkably, it can still be purchased at a massive discount to its intrinsic value. This favorable combination of attributes is extremely rare, particularly for a large-cap company.

UBER is investing heavily in AI-powered autonomous driving technology, forming partnerships with AI tech firm Wayve and Nvidia (NVDA) among others. “Generative AI will power the future of mobility, requiring both rich data and very powerful compute,” UBER CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said in January. “By working with NVIDIA, we are confident that we can help supercharge the timeline for safe and scalable autonomous driving solutions for the industry.”

UBER Stock Quant Breakdown

Ackman is right regarding UBER’s valuation: its forward PEG of 0.55 comes at a near 61% discount to the 1.48 sector median. As SA analyst YR research noted mid-March:

At $72 a share, Uber trades at 29 times forward earnings, and 21 times '26 estimates. It's also trading at 26 times free cash flows (adjusted for SBC). I challenge you to find a non-cyclical company, growing revenues at a mid-teens pace, with a significant margin expansion trajectory, that's trading at a more attractive valuation.

The rideshare group boasts fantastic growth metrics, including ‘A+’ EPS GAAP growth of 56%, a 450% difference from the 10% sector median. ‘A+’ profitability is lifted higher by return on common equity of 60% (a 371% difference vs. the sector) and $7.1B in cash from operations.

UBER also maintains strong momentum; its nearly flat three-month price performance is still an impressive feat compared to the -15% sector median. My next selection has suffered a blow from the market sell-off but remains resilient thanks to sector-leading fundamentals.

3. Celestica Inc. (NYSE: CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $8.2B

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 13 out of 543

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 1 out of 16

Also included among my Top AI Stocks for March, Celestica (CLS) manufactures critical electronic parts for data centers. The group has dominated a lucrative portion of the AI supply chain as tech companies’ insatiable demand for hyperscalers continues to be a powerful growth tailwind in volatile markets. A two-time Alpha Pick, the Toronto-based company is down 20% year to date but has generated more than 178% for the Alpha Picks portfolio since being added in October 2023.

Celestica Inc. 1-year Trading Chart (as of 4/8/2025)

CLS reported expansive revenue growth in Q4 powered by surging AI demand and expanding margins, with adjusted EPS of $1.11, beating analysts’ expectations of $1.06. While tariffs have undoubtedly weighed on CLS’s celestial rise, its strengthening balance sheet and powerful growth outlook should see it through the bear market. As SA analyst Star Investments wrote over the weekend:

Despite some backward valuations appearing high, forward-looking valuation ratios and peer comparisons indicate CLS may be undervalued, especially considering its growth prospects…Tariffs and a potential trade war may continue to negatively impact investor sentiment, and the stock may continue to decline over the near and medium term.

The Canadian group forecasts $10.7B in revenue and 22% EPS growth for 2025. While that may seem like an ambitious goal, explosive AI and data center demand reiterate SA Quant’s Strong Buy rating as the largest U.S. tech companies continue pouring hundreds of billions into the sector.

CLS Stock Quant Breakdown

CLS’s valuation has only grown more attractive as the selloff has dragged down its share price, improving from a ‘C+’ to a ‘B+’ over the past three months. Its forward PEG has decreased from 0.8 to 0.47 since I rolled out last month’s top AI picks, now representing a 64% discount to the sector median. Its forward P/E of 15.2 comes at a 32% discount.

CLS earns gold stars in terms of growth and profitability, with forward EPS GAAP Growth of 38.6% (a 240.07% difference from the sector) and 23% return on common equity (TTM) (a 413.8% difference) among other positive metrics. And while tariffs may have taken a temporary toll, CLS’s star power continues to shine through with strong momentum.

Finally, 10 positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days suggest that Wall Street analysts share my continued faith in this AI innovator. Like CLS, this next stock has charted a celestial rise in the AI universe by carving a unique niche in the sector.

4. FARO Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ: FARO)

  • Market Capitalization: $458.41M

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Equipment and Instruments

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 10 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 2 out of 47

Also included within my top 5 AI stocks for March, Faro Technologies (FARO) is a Lake Mary, Florida-based maker of 3D measuring and scanning technology, leveraging AI to become a leader in “smart factories” and “intelligent automation.” Despite losing around 15% over the past month, FARO’s strong balance sheet and excellent momentum have kept it a top pick of mine.

The company achieved double-digit EBITDA margins for the first time in a decade in 2024, propelled by soaring demand for global 3D scanning—a market poised to grow to $11.85B by 2032 at a CAGR of 13.11%.

Its strong fundamentals, detailed below, reinforce its future growth potential.

FARO Stock Quant Breakdown

FARO’s overall 'B+' growth grade is backed by forward EBIT growth of 112.48%—a 1,410.71% difference from the sector median—and Y/Y levered FCF of 30%, 103% above the 14.8% sector median. Forward EBITDA growth of 42.76%, a 639.9% sector from the sector median, foreshadows more growth to follow. Robust momentum is reflected by six-month price performance of 33%, eclipsing the -20% sector median. Even as its shares struggle in the current market, three-month price performance of -10% still beats the sector’s -26%.

Despite its overall 'C-' valuation, FARO still offers an attractive discount to many of its peers. Its enterprise value-to-sales (FWD) and price-to-sales ratio (FWD) are both around 1.3, representing around a 45% discount from the sector median each.

Like FARO, my final selection has supercharged its growth with the power of AI.

5. Upstart Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ: UPST)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.5B

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 84 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 11 out of 39

Upstart Technologies (UPST) is a cloud-based AI lending platform that offers personal loans, automotive retail and refinance loans, home equity lines of credit, and small dollar loans. Headquartered in San Mateo, California, the fintech group has ridden the wave of strong private credit demand, crushing expectations in Q4 with EPS of $0.26 when Wall Street expected it to lose 4 cents per share. The company also reported incredible sales growth in the fourth quarter, soaring 56% from Q4 2023, while total 2024 sales grew an impressive 24% Y/Y.

The company’s AI-powered underwriting algorithms have also exhibited improvement, allowing it to approve more loans at lower APRs and thus allowing its bank partners to allocate more capital at a higher rate of return. As a result, the company expects to grow its revenues by 57% in 2025, a far higher margin than other fintech lenders. As SA analyst Julian Lin recently wrote:

Management has given strong full-year guidance which implies an acceleration in top-line growth even as the company begins lapping tougher comparables. The company appears poised to return to GAAP profitability, which may also help to offer further support to the valuation.

UPST’s financial improvements have also begun to reflect in its Quant Factor Grades, detailed below.

UPST Stock Quant Breakdown

While SA’s Quant System does not currently display UPST’s PEG, we can estimate the stock’s 2026 PEG by taking its forward 2026 P/E of 15.26 and dividing that by analysts’s estimated 2026 EPS growth of 64.21% (or 64.21, for the purpose of this calculation). 15.26/64.21=0.24. Should analysts’ projections remain intact, a PEG of 0.24 would likely represent an attractive discount from the financial sector median, currently valued at 1.03.

UPST Earnings Estimates

Forward revenue growth of 34%, a 457% difference from the 6.1% sector median, and a levered free cash flow margin (TTM) of 38.4%, a 94.6% difference, reflect solid growth and profitability. Momentum has stalled in recent months given the market sell-off. However, 12 upward EPS revisions from analysts over the past 90 days, 11 upward revenue revisions, and 0 negative revisions in either category reinforce SA Quant’s Strong Buy rating.

Conclusion

Donald Trump’s tariffs have unleashed rampant volatility throughout the markets. While uncertainty will likely continue over the coming months, the market always returns to fundamentals. Instead of selling in fear, consider stocks with strong balance sheets and Quant Factor Grades that are well-prepared to generate returns once conditions stabilize.

Explore AI investing with these five Strong Buys, or go deeper using Seeking Alpha's ETF Ratings Screener tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, consider Alpha Picks if you're seeking a limited number of Seeking Alpha Quant’s best monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks.

data.title: Reducing Risk As Uncertainty Bites
data.description:
The sharp escalation in global trade tensions and extreme trade policy uncertainty has triggered a broad risk asset selloff. It is less clear if uncertainty will cloud the outlook for a little or a lot longer, so we reduce our tactical horizon to three months. That means giving more weight to our early view that risk assets could face more near-term pressure. We reduce equity exposure for now and allocate more to short-term U.S. Treasuries that could benefit as investors seek refuge from volatility.

The escalating trade conflict sparked the worst one-week selloff in the S&P 500 and U.S. high yield bonds since the 2020 pandemic shock – even as U.S. economic conditions remain solid, as seen in strong U.S. jobs data. We expected risk assets would remain under pressure until uncertainty starts to dissipate – and it’s now less clear over how long or short a period policy uncertainty could cloud the outlook. We now see a bigger growth drag and inflation boost. The Federal Reserve also expects a bigger impact than it did in March. See the chart. Many countries are preparing responses to U.S. tariffs. China has kicked off with 34% tariffs and other measures. Yet the full set of international responses and country-specific negotiations with the U.S. will take time, making it hard to have visibility on when and how this will settle. Major wealth destruction could hurt sentiment and consumer spending.

We still believe U.S. stocks will eventually reclaim global leadership due to mega forces – like the buildout and adoption of artificial intelligence. But for now, we shorten our tactical horizon to three months and reduce risk. A shorter tactical horizon means giving more weight to our early view that risk assets could stay under near-term pressure until uncertainty starts to dissipate. That’s why we reduce equity exposure, including to U.S. and Chinese stocks, and allocate more to short-term U.S. Treasuries that could benefit as investors seek refuge from volatility. If clarity comes quickly, we would up risk-taking again.

Getting selective

While we are more cautious about broad benchmarks, sharp selloffs are creating opportunities for security selection. U.S. policy shifts are spurring fiscal spending globally. In Europe, Germany’s €1 trillion package for defense and infrastructure investment is creating opportunities in the defense sector. We don’t think the growth and earnings outlook yet supports sustained European equity outperformance but a broader macro opportunity could emerge if the European Union finds a way to jointly issue bonds to fund investment across the bloc, as it did in the pandemic.

Overall, we think plans for a new wave of U.S. tariffs and responses from other countries reinforce that we will be in a world where interest rates – and long-term bond yields – stay higher than pre-pandemic. Tariffs and looser fiscal policy in some parts of the world will likely push up on inflation. We lean against market pricing of four to five quarter-point rate cuts by the Fed this year: U.S. core inflation is tracking well above the Fed’s 2% target, even before the impact of new tariffs.

We stay underweight long-term Treasuries given persistent U.S. deficits and sticky core inflation. We expect investors to demand more term premium, or compensation for holding long-term bonds given sticky inflation, higher-for-longer interest rates and a tough fiscal outlook. That could put upward pressure on long-term U.S. Treasury yields. We think gold can serve as a better return diversifier in this environment.

Our bottom line

Trade tensions have triggered a risk asset selloff. We see volatility persisting for some time, so we shorten our tactical horizon to three months and reduce risk-taking, turning neutral on U.S. equities and preferring short-term Treasuries.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Shopify Employees Are Getting More Done In Less Time
data.description:
Introduction

Per my November article, Shopify’s (NASDAQ:SHOP) business has excellent prospects. The Q4 2024 filings have come out since then, and the seasonal holiday numbers were outstanding. Additionally, we now have access to an April memo from CEO Tobi Lütke. My thesis is that Shopify employees are getting more work done in less time, which leads to operating income growing faster than revenue.

The Numbers

In May 2023, Shopify said they were selling the majority of their logistics business and reducing the workforce by 23%. The 2024 10-K has a “Reduction in Workforce” note which talks about this headcount reduction:

In May 2023, the Company reduced headcount by approximately 23% of employees across the Company ("2023 Reduction in Workforce"). The Company incurred and paid $148 million in total severance related costs in the year ended December 31, 2023.

Given the sale of the logistics business along with efficiency improvements in the e-commerce business, Shopify’s revenue per employee has gone up dramatically:

Parkinson’s Law is a book mentioned repeatedly by CEO Lütke and one of its points is that work expands to fill the time available for its completion. As such, CEO Lütke has always been wary about the headcount becoming disproportionate, but now he knows his employees have AI available to them which should take productivity up to the next level. The April memo from CEO Lütke explains the opportunities for Shopify’s employees in terms of using AI:

as a thought partner, deep researcher, critic, tutor, or pair programmer.

CEO Lütke goes on to say AI acts as a multiplier:

We also learned that, as opposed to most tools, AI acts as a multiplier. We are all lucky to work with some amazing colleagues, the kind who contribute 10X of what was previously thought possible. It’s my favorite thing about this company. And what’s even more amazing is that, for the first time, we see the tools become 10X themselves. I’ve seen many of these people approach implausible tasks, ones we wouldn’t even have chosen to tackle before, with reflexive and brilliant usage of AI to get 100X the work done.

The memo makes it clear that AI is now a fundamental expectation of all employees at Shopify, and it is a part of the prototype phase of any get S--t done (“GSD”) project. AI should enable Shopify to keep the headcount close to flat as revenue continues growing. This means operating expenses will continue to drop as a percentage of revenue. This is because stock-based compensation ("SBC") overwhelmingly hits operating expense lines as opposed to the cost of revenue line.

Valuation

We have to understand the future prospects of growth in terms of volume moving through Shopify’s ecosystem in order to make cash flow estimates about the future. Shopify’s volume growth has been incredible, even against powerful online companies like Amazon (AMZN). We’re seeing more volume growth in Shopify channels than in brick and mortar channels like Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT). Shopify Y/Y GMV growth has crushed Amazon 1P sales growth, and it has been more than estimated Amazon 3P GMV growth for every period from Q1 2019 to present except for Q3 2022, Q4 2022, Q1 2023 and Q2 2023:

I think of the rule of 40 with respect to valuation and Shopify did well with it for the year seeing as their 2024 revenue growth was 26% and their 2024 FCF margin was 18%. The sum of these considerations was well over 40% and not many companies in the world are able to beat this benchmark.

Shopify is worth the amount of cash we can pull out of it from now until judgment day. Management talks about it being a 100-year company, so hopefully judgment day is far away! One factor I like to think about with respect to valuation is the breakdown by geography. The 2024 10-K says the US has 45% of the merchants and 64% of the revenue. 30% of the merchants are in EMEA, but that segment only contributes 19% of the revenue. APAC has 15% of the merchants and 10% of the revenue. 5% of the merchants and 6% of the revenue are from Canada. Latin America has 5% of the merchants but just 1% of the revenue.

The 10-K divides cohorts by year to show it isn’t just new merchants contributing to revenue growth. More sales from old merchants more than offset merchants who leave Shopify because of going out of business or switching to another operating system:

The total annual revenue generated by each of our previous cohorts has grown on a generally consistent basis.

The 10-K makes this easy to visualize, and Shopify did an excellent job of helping their 2023 merchants increase volume during 2024 (increased volume drives higher revenue):

The Q1 2024 release says the following about Q1 2025 expectations (emphasis added):

• Revenue to grow at a mid-twenties percentage rate on a year-over-year basis;

• Gross profit dollars to grow at a low-twenties percentage rate on a year-over-year basis;

• Operating expense as a percentage of revenue to be 41% to 42%;

• Stock-based compensation to be $120 million; and

• Free cash flow margin to be in the mid-teens.

Operating expenses were 47% of revenue in Q1 2024, so if they are 41% to 42% of revenue in Q1 2025, then this is a huge improvement! If Q1 2025 revenue is 25% higher than the Q1 2024 figure of $1,861 million, then it should be about $2,325 million when rounded to the nearest $5 million. If we have a FCF margin of about 15% then Q1 2025 FCF should be about $350 million relative to $232 million for Q1 2024. If improvement can continue throughout the year, then I am optimistic we can see FCF of about $2.25 billion for 2025. Before the changes with tariffs from last week, I wouldn’t argue with investors who use a valuation range of 50 to 60x this amount, which is $115 to $135 billion when we round to the nearest $5 billion.

Things are different now. We know 64% of Shopify’s revenue comes from the U.S., and much of this is from products which were manufactured outside of the U.S. I believe we have to use lower multiples until or unless uncertainty with tariffs goes away. I now think a range of 40 to 50x 2025 FCF is more reasonable, which is $90 to $115 billion when rounded to the nearest $5 billion.

Per the 2024 10-K, there were 1,215,528,049 Class A shares plus 79,292,685 Class B shares as of February 7 for a total consideration of 1,294,820,734 shares. Multiplying by the April 7 share price of $78.82 gives us a market cap of about $102 billion. The stock is inside my valuation range, and I think it is a hold for long-term investors who intend to hold it for at least three years.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.

data.title: Kobenhavns Lufthavne: Wait For The Buy Offer From The Danish State
data.description:
Airport infrastructure is one of the areas that I cover. My coverage includes airports in Mexico, but also Europe, Asia, and Oceania. In this report, I am adding Københavns Lufthavne A/S (OTC:CPNHF), or Copenhagen Airport, to my coverage.

Unfortunately, my initiation of coverage comes rather late. In December, the Danish State agreed to acquire a controlling stake in the airport which would bring the ownership by the State to 98%. In response, the stock jumped 60%. Upon completion of the acquisition, the Danish State will make an offer to shareholders for the remaining minority stake as was reported on the 8th of April.

Copenhagen Airport: A 30 Million Passenger Airport

Københavns Lufthavne A/S does not just include the airport of Copenhagen, as it also includes Roskilde Airport. However, the latter is a very small airport handling general aviation and private jet flights.

In 2024, the airport handled 29.9 million passengers, which is very close to the per-pandemic flow of 30.3 million passengers. The airport is the hub of Scandinavian Airlines, while Norwegian Air Shuttle ASA (OTCPK:NWARF) has an operating base at the airport.

Copenhagen Airport has increased its capacity since 2014 through upgrades of Terminals 2 and 3 and a new concourse called Pier E, while the remaining Piers have been expanded and updated. This multi-year plan will support growth towards a capacity of 40 million passengers each year later this decade.

Strong Growth For Copenhagen Airport In 2024

In 2024, Copenhagen Airport booked revenue growth of nearly 25% and EBITDA grew 53%, and there actually is not a lot to say about that growth. Passenger traffic grew 11% and so the remainder of the growth was driven by higher unit revenues.

The higher aeronautical revenues fully passed through to the EBITDA level resulting in a significant increase in EBITDA. EBITDA margins stood at 50.8%. That is a solid performance. I also cover Aeroports de Paris SA (OTCPK:AEOXF) which operates the Parisian airports and that group has an EBITDA margin of 33.5% while Aena S.M.E., S.A. (OTCPK:ANNSF) has margins of 60%. So, Copenhagen Airport is not the top performer, but it is definitely not at the bottom, either, and I do believe that higher passenger traffic in the years ahead should further drive margins.

Wait For The Offer From The Danish State

Normally, the growth prospect of Copenhagen Airport would be attractive for investment. There are always risks such as GDP growth stalling which could hurt the growth of passenger traffic at airports and there is little that airports can do in that regard. So, that is a risk next to weather disruptions and global shocks. However, at this point, there is no reason to buy the stock.

Copenhagen Airport is a company that actually does not have a lot of free float. There are 7.85 million shares but only 1.4% are traded by investors. The Danish state owns 39.2%, and 59.4% is held by OTPP and ATP. That stake has been valued at 32 billion kroner, or DKK 6,864, per share. The stock price currently is DKK 6,460, which would provide a 6% upside if the Danish State makes the same offer to private and institutional investors.

To me, that is not enough to justify a Buy rating. You could buy shares at the Nasdaq Copenhagen waiting for the offer, but I am not particularly thrilled about holding a stock for a 6% upside.

Conclusion: Keep Holding Copenhagen Airport Stock

On any normal day, I would probably issue a Buy rating for Copenhagen Airport, as it has made investments to increase capacity to 40 million passengers annually, providing an additional 33% growth from current passenger numbers. However, with the Danish State acquiring the 59.4% stake from OTPP and ATP, the only reason to keep holding this stock is to capitalize on the Danish State making an offer for the remaining 1.4% of the shares.

I do expect this to happen - the State to make the offer - but that will happen at the moment the transfer of the shares held by ATP and OTPP will also be completed. Until that time, the stock is a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ambev: A Strong Past, A Weak Future
data.description:
Undoubtedly, Ambev's history is something that makes any Brazilian proud. It all started with a bank acquiring a struggling brewery, turning it around, and expanding through acquisitions. This trajectory transformed the small Brazilian brewery into the world's largest brewing company: AB InBev (BUD). For those unfamiliar, BUD as we know it today is the result of the hard work of three Brazilians-Marcel Telles, Beto Sicupira, and Jorge Paulo Lemann-the trio that challenged global giants and consolidated themselves as the largest brewing company in the world.

In this process, Ambev became one of AB InBev's key operations, focused on the South American and Canadian markets, with its largest footprint in Brazil.

All of this growth made Ambev one of the greatest investment opportunities of all time, creating many millionaires. However, that is now in the past.

For over 10 years, the company has remained stagnant, rapidly losing market share, suffering from cost pressures, and being unable to pass on price increases, resulting in declining margins. The sum of these factors, as we'll see throughout this article, explains why I believe Ambev is a great SELL opportunity.

Introduction

Formed from the merger of two traditional Brazilian brands, Brahma and Antarctica, Ambev (NYSE:ABEV) became one of the world's leading breweries, even before the creation of BUD. As the company thrived, it expanded until, in 2004, Ambev merged with Interbrew. Following the merger, Ambev became a subsidiary of the group, which is now called AB InBev and trades under the ticker BUD.

Ambev's operations are mainly concentrated in South America (76% of EBITDA), but it also operates in Central America and Canada.

Its product portfolio includes several market-leading brands, primarily beers, but it also serves the non-alcoholic beverage market and operates two distribution software platforms: (i) B2C - Zé Delivery (ii) B2B - Bees.

Financial History

With several acquisitions over its history, Ambev experienced rapid growth, reaching nearly USD 14 billion in revenue in 2010 (vs USD 2 billion in 2002, almost a 6x increase).

As the brand grew, Ambev strengthened its relationships with suppliers, increasing payment terms and reducing receivables collection time, which is normal for a market leader. This allowed the company to become a real cash machine, as its operations were effectively financed by its suppliers.

This advantage helped solidify the company's absolute leadership in Brazil, reaching 70% market share.

However, this success story began to change in the early 2010s.

Being dominant in all its markets, the company grew less through acquisitions, resulting in stagnant revenues from 2013 to the present.

So why did this happen? Over the past decade, the beer market in Brazil has changed significantly, with the emergence of many new breweries and the rapid growth of Heineken.

Since 2013, the number of breweries in Brazil has increased from 195 to over 1,800-a compound annual growth rate of 25%.

In parallel, the number of different beers has grown even faster, reaching over 45,000 types.

This growth of the so-called "Craft Breweries" in Brazil, along with increased investment from Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY)-including the acquisition of Brasil Kirin and production capacity expansion-led Ambev to lose significant market share, shedding more than 10 percentage points by 2023.

"With an investment of more than BRL 1.5 billion supported by the State Government through the Paraná Competitive Program, the company more than doubled the factory's production capacity, enabling the inclusion of new labels in Heineken Brazil's portfolio."

As a consequence of rising competition, Ambev has faced increasing pressure to absorb cost hikes, having to sacrifice part of its gross margin to maintain market share. As a result, there's a continued trend of margin compression.

Revenue stagnation combined with margin deterioration led to a 45% drop in operating profit over the past 11 years.

Still, despite these challenges, the company continues to generate free cash flow, achieving a 9% FCF yield in 2024. With this strong cash generation, Ambev has been increasing its dividend distribution, reaching 5.8% last year and building up cash reserves, which totaled USD 4.2 billion at the end of 2024 (~11% of its market cap).

While a 5.8% dividend may be considered attractive in the U.S., it's less so in Brazil, where many companies offer yields above 10%, as shown below:

Investment Thesis

As seen above, although Ambev is an outstanding company, it has been facing several challenges that have reduced its growth and profitability. On the other hand, its stagnated profile means the company doesn't need to invest heavily in Capex, resulting in high cash flow to shareholders and an excellent capital structure.

If the company is so good, why recommend a SELL? The answer is simple: (i) A declining business and (ii) Opportunity cost.

(i) As previously mentioned, Ambev is no longer a growth company. It now fits the profile of a low-growth or even declining business. As Peter Lynch says, it doesn't make much sense to hold a stock in this stage unless it pays high dividends-which is not the case here. To be considered a good dividend payer (compared to alternatives in Brazil), the yield should exceed 10%. For this to happen, the stock should be trading near BRL 8 (USD 1.36).

(ii) Brazil's macroeconomic challenges have pushed stock valuations-excluding Petrobras and Vale-to one of the lowest price/earnings levels in recent years, at 9.18x. Meanwhile, Ambev is trading at a 15x multiple-a high level for a company that is not growing its earnings.

Assuming the average P/E multiple of the Brazilian market, the fair value for Ambev would be BRL 8.03 (USD 1.38).

Thus, we have two distinct scenarios suggesting that Ambev's shares should be valued at USD 1.37, representing a 40% downside.

As a counterargument, let's imagine that Ambev shares are indeed cheap. If the stock were to return to its recent peak in 2018, at BRL 24 (USD 4.11, based on the current exchange rate), what would the multiples be? The market cap would be USD 65 billion. The P/E ratio would be 27x, EV/EBITDA at 14x, and dividend yield at 2.6%.

These numbers don't seem feasible considering the company's recent track record.

Therefore, we see that the shares are currently expensive, even taking into account the large cash reserves. Looking ahead, uncertainty is high, with declining beer consumption among younger generations, increased competition, and rising commodity prices reducing Brazilians' purchasing power.

To finalize the valuation, I performed a discounted cash flow analysis. For the assumptions, I used 3% annual growth and 1.5% in perpetuity, aligned with population growth forecasts.

For working capital, Capex, and depreciation, I used the average of recent years. For net margin, I used a value 3 percentage points above that presented in 2024.

The discount rate is a mix of Brazil's long-term fixed interest rate (~15%) and a risk premium (2%). If I were to consider a more reasonable risk premium (5%), the discount rate would be at least 20%.

Conclusion

Ambev's quality and historical success-especially in its early years-are undeniable. However, in terms of valuation, the three methodologies presented prove that Ambev shares are overvalued, with downside potential.

Using an average of the approaches, my price target is USD 1.76 (24% downside). Using a discount rate consistent with Brazil (~20%), the average of the approaches would lead to a target price of USD 1.64 (30% downside).

Based on other comments I've read, many SA subscribers don't agree with a sell recommendation, often due to the "high dividend yield." But it's important to point out that Ambev is not a U.S. company-it is Brazilian. In this case, it should be compared to other Brazilian companies.

As we've seen, Ambev trades at a significant premium vs. peers in the index (excluding Petrobras (PBR) and Vale (VALE)).

Finally, if you want exposure to the Brazilian market, an option is to SELL Ambev shares and buy the Brazilian market ETF, such as EWZ or EWZS. Over the past 5 years, this strategy has outperformed, and if Brazil resolves its political issues, it could outperform again in the future.

data.title: CNO Financial Group: I Am Cautious Of The Immediate Future
data.description:
Introduction

CNO Financial Group, Inc. (NYSE:CNO) had a great 2024. However, with so much uncertainty regarding the global economy, I don’t think we will see a repeat of that in 2025 unless everything I mentioned is overblown, which I don’t think it is. Nevertheless, I remain cautious.

Briefly on Performance

Looking at the company’s top-line performance for 2024, we can see that Q4 saw a slight slowdown in growth compared to the previous quarters. The slight decline in revenues can be attributed to the company realizing some investment losses and adjustments on unrealized investments to losses for the quarter. Otherwise, the company saw some growth in Insurance policy income, up about 3%. The company’s investments of around $28B generated a gain of $399.5m for the quarter also, which is around 23% gain y/y.

Regarding the company’s profitability, margins across the board took a hit in Q3 ’24 but are seen to be recovering since Q4 ’24. EBT was favorably affected by a -21% decline in total benefits and expenses line item, primarily from a decrease in insurance policy benefits. The company attributed this overall improvement to tighter claims management, pricing discipline, and the improvements in underwriting margins.

On the company’s ROE for the year, we can see a similar situation unfolding. As the company’s bottom line started to improve at the end of the year, the company’s return on equity followed suit. At the end of the year, the company’s return on equity improved to around 17%, which is above industry average and well above the insurance industry average by a few points. The company emphasizes its operating return on equity rather than GAAP ROE; however, given that its GAAP ROE is much higher than the average, an operating ROE of 10% is also going to lead the pack. It seems to me that the management is doing a great job at running the company efficiently by utilizing shareholder capital very well, thus creating value.

Now, let’s look at the company’s financial situation. CNO finished the year with around $2B in cash and equivalents, against $4.5B in debt, which is typical of an insurance company to have quite a bit of debt on its books. Is it worrisome? I don’t think so, there is plenty of cash on the books, as well as the company is making a decent profit every quarter, which covers interest expenses and will be able to cover for a long while.

Overall, the company ended the year with a decent upswing in operations, suggesting we may have seen the bottom. Top-line growth is still struggling slightly, however, the company’s profitability more than offset the lackluster top line. It doesn’t appear to me that the company is overleveraged at the moment either.

Comments on the Outlook

In the latest conference call, the management was rather confident about the future of 2025 in terms of operations. They expect to make between $3.70 and $3.90 of operating earnings, as well as improve operating ROE by 50bps to 10.50%. The performance of the company in 2024 supports further improvements in 2025. NAP rose 7% for the year, while Medicare Supplement NAP saw a 26% increase. It seems that there was a massive surge in demand for the company's services, as submitted policies went up 39% in the quarter.

The company expects further improvements in efficiency and profitability from the modernization of the company’s ecosystem, which will take advantage of the new advanced technology like AI and cloud. It will, of course, come with some expenses in the short run for the long-term gain.

Since the latest conference call came out three months ago now, I expect there will be a lot of changes in the talking points, especially regarding the outlook for 2025. The recession was mentioned once and rather brushed off because Paul McDonough, the CFO of the company, said that the ranges of the cash flow will depend on the economic climate, and expect to see lower ranges if we “tip into a recession.” That is all. The current cash flow ranges from $200m to $250m for 2025, which I don’t think takes into account an actual recession.

Now, the prevalent topic of the last month or two is the recession that may be caused by the unprecedented tariffs the Trump Administration implemented globally less than a week ago, which rocked and continue to rock the financial markets with volatility unseen in many years. Recently, Goldman Sachs (GS) updated its odds of the U.S. recession to 45% from 35% just last week. All because of tariffs and the trade war that may soon ensue if other countries begin to retaliate, which will lead the U.S. to raise tariffs even further. JPMorgan (JPM) is even more of a doomsayer, as they put the odds of a U.S. and global recession at 60%.

What does a recession mean for a company like CNO? Consumers typically cut back on discretionary spending, which includes insurance premiums. Middle-income consumers, which is CNO’s primary market, may allow their policies to be unpaid and lapse in order to save money in tough times, which will lead to a decline in the company’s operations.

In terms of business impact, many smaller companies may start to struggle in tougher economic conditions, which will lead to a reduction or outright cancellation of worksite benefits. All this will be a negative for the company as it will collect less premium, which may lead to a reduction in capital spending as growth will be affected.

I am also expecting to see some losses on the company’s investments this upcoming quarter because many of the markets briefly entered bear territory, and we know general returns on investments play a huge role in the company’s overall top-line performance. So, if all this happens, the company may reduce efforts of technology upgrades to maintain profitability. Expenses will need to be reduced, and projects of growth will be delayed.

The company will have to do all it can to retain its customers in such difficult times. New customers will be hard to come by in a tough economic climate, so CNO may have to become a bit more flexible in its payment options to keep client retention high, which may eat into its operations and profitability in the short term until the downturn passes.

In short, I expect to hear some updates in the upcoming Q1 ’25 conference call regarding the numbers given back in Q4 ’24, which may be updated or at least the management will be talking more cautiously regarding the 2025 outlook, which already has a lot more risks of recession than it did just a few months ago.

Closing Comments

I cannot take what the company said back in February at face value about its outlook any longer. So much has changed regarding the economy that I believe there is a high chance that the management will be approaching the situation with a lot more caution going forward and will not be as optimistic as they were just a few months ago.

Now, we may not see a recession in 2025, and Trump’s tariffs are reversed, which is not unusual since he has been flip-flopping before; however, if these remain, there is no winning and only more uncertainty in the short run, especially how the everyday person is going to be affected around the world, not just the U.S.

The company is due to report its Q1 ’25 at the end of April, so I will be tuning in to hear what the management has to say and what kind of tone we are going to hear regarding the immediate future. For now, I will remain on the sidelines.

data.title: Apple Sell-Off: A Massive Overreaction To Tariff News
data.description:
This morning, I read a wonderful news article by Seeking Alpha News Editor Chris Ciaccia on Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), and I wanted to chime in as I know many investors are very worried when they saw the following happen to their Apple shares over the last five days.

I know this was quite a shock to most investors as Apple is usually considered a flight to quality stock, where scared investors typically run to in times of trouble, but that was not the case this time around. I had prepared for this situation some time ago and already had my clients and subscribers in a large position of cash, but despite all the preparation, I did not expect the panic sell-off that occurred in the last three trading days to be so quick and nasty.

Nevertheless, being in a large position in cash before the “Liberation Day” event, we are only down a fraction of what the markets are. Unfortunately, I am expecting these problems to continue for some time going forward as earnings season will not be pretty, as CEO’s will mostly issue negative guidance, due to the fact that no one knows how these tariffs will play out. That will add additional risk into the equation.

The sell-off in Apple shares is a complete and total overreaction, especially when shoppers are flooding the Apple stores right now in order to buy up the current inventory in droves, prior to the tariffs kicking in. The weak dollar will also boost Apple's earnings when they repatriate revenues from overseas. On December 19, 2019, President Trump gave a waiver to Apple when he slapped tariffs on China then, and I am expecting him to do the same thing this time around as well, as Apple is planning to invest $500 billion in the USA and President Trump loves Apple for doing that. Once the waiver kicks in, Apple shares should soar, in my opinion, so selling this jewel would be incredibly stupid right now. How do I know this? Well, in January 2019, I wrote a very similar titled article here on Seeking Alpha and you should clearly see that this is basically history repeating itself for Apple's shares.

Here is the chart of Apple then.

Clearly these types of events can happen quite often, even to the elite companies in the world. Luckily for me, Seeking Alpha allows us to see the actual performance that was achieved by Apple since that article was published.

The panic selling in 2019 created a tremendous buying opportunity for those who bought them and followed my Strong Buy rating at the time. We are also lucky in that I am running the same Seeking Alpha Subscription service that I was running then, and I can update the datafile as well as the charts to compare what you would have seen then, to the new ones from today, using our Friedrich Datafiles and Charts algorithm.

January 2019

April 2025

January 2019

April 2025

The charts, first of all, are rather similar as the Wall Street prices (White Lines) are moving in the same direction except the 2019 one broke below our Bargain Price (Green Lines) while the new one still has room to fall, before it becomes a Strong Buy at $155.35. But what you are getting now is a company that on Main Street is twice as powerful as it was in 2019 as its FROIC then was 28%, and today it is 66%. FROIC = FREE CASH FLOW RETURN ON INVESTED CAPITAL, and basically, Apple returns 66 cents in free cash flow for every $1 of invested capital. This makes it amazingly more profitable than what one bought in 2019. This can be attributed to improved production and lower costs, as it benefits from economies of scale. Economies of scale are a cost-saving benefit that occurs when a company increases its production volume. This happens because fixed costs are spread out over more units produced.

That can be seen by using our CAPFLOW Ratio (Capital Expenditures/Cash Flow) which went from 19% to 9%, so elite management got twice the bang for the buck! How this may change if Apple moves production to the USA is still unknown because in China, the company does not pay health benefits, payroll taxes or real estate taxes. Those are added costs in the USA, and hourly wage costs could be $30 an hour vs. $3 in China, India or Vietnam. That problem could be eliminated if robotics was used instead of workers, as you have no payroll taxes or health benefits. That is the only way any company coming to the USA to produce its products, can keep the same margins it is getting in China.

Going forward, what President Trump is doing is a global paradigm shift and that will not be easy to implement for the companies pledging to build plants here. But if anyone can do it, the elite management team at Apple, led by CEO Tim Cook, should figure it out. We currently, like Seeking Alpha Research, are ranking Apple as a Strong Hold.

data.title: DigitalBridge: Sustained Collapse Sows Uncertainty About Digital Infrastructure Assets
data.description:
DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (NYSE:DBRG) is down 37% year-to-date to cap off a torrid start to 2025. This held the promise of a ramp in fee-earning equity under management ("FEEUM") with DBRG pushing to expand this to $40 billion at the end of the year, up at least $6 billion from FEEUM of $36 billion as of the end of the fiscal 2024 fourth quarter. The asset manager raised $9 billion in new fee-paying capital in 2024, exceeding its target to raise $7 billion, as demand for digital infrastructure assets and businesses continued to ramp up during the year. DBRG's investment focus on digital infrastructure as an asset class has formed the core part of its investment profile, but total returns for the stock have been dire on the back of consistent losses.

DBRG generated fourth quarter revenue of $66.17 million, down 81.1% over its year-ago comp and a miss by $27.77 million on consensus estimates. The asset manager declared a peppercorn cash dividend of $0.01 per share, $0.04 per share annually for a 0.56% dividend yield. FEEUM grew by 8% year-over-year in 2024. Critically, DBRG at its current $1.32 billion market cap is materially down versus its historical trading range, reflecting a macro environment where its core appeal to institutional investors, including insurance companies, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, and family offices, is markedly dimmed on capital flight to haven and highly liquid assets.

Risks, Growth, And The Trendline

We saw the US 10-year Treasury yield collapse in recent days following "Liberation Day" as capital rushed towards the asset class to act as a hedge against the material level of uncertainty currently faced by the US economy. There have been different tranches of tariffs already implemented, from auto tariffs, liberation day tariffs, and steel tariffs, with some further tariffs also pending, including a levy on Chinese-built ships docking in US ports. The downstream impact of this is undoubtedly inflationary, perhaps only slightly countered by the collapse in oil prices.

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model's latest estimate for the first quarter of 2025 GDP growth is negative 2.8%, down from positive economic growth north of 2% as recently as January. This comes as the one-year US inflation outlook in March hit 5% according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers. The five-year implied inflation expectation came in at 4.3%, up 80 basis points versus the prior month. Hence, the core risk for DBRG and the core driver of the collapse in the asset manager's valuation year-to-date is the confluence of what has been historically parallel factors of inflation and recession, the combination of both will be extremely destructive to capital raising efforts, and forms the core metric for both DBRG bulls and bears to monitor their position.

DBRG is now trading at its lowest-ever level, even with its FEEUM at the highest it has ever been for the digital infrastructure alternative asset manager. The market is looking towards a potentially recessionary macro environment, in aggregate with rising inflation and a paralyzed Fed, as reasons to sell DBRG and dampen expectations that the company will be able to meet its target for 2025 FEEUM growth. However, bulls should continue to anchor their hopes on DBRG's aggregate investments and businesses with long-term contracted cash flows, investment-grade customers, and predictable yields across its verticals from data centers, small cell networks, edge infrastructure, macro cell towers, and fiber networks.

DBRG earned fee revenue of $330 million during the fourth quarter on a trailing 12-month basis, up $27 million sequentially, with fee-related earnings hitting $107 million during the quarter, up $9 million sequentially. A potential reversal of this could be in the cards, lining up DBRG for what would possibly be its first decline in FRE if the US were to fall into a recession and a broad appeal for digital infrastructure and liquidity were to materially fall in exchange for US treasuries. While I held a position in the commons, this was closed this month, with the preferreds offering a safer investment option with a markedly higher yield. DBRG has three preferreds: the 7.125% Series H (NYSE:DBRG.PR.H), 7.15% Series I (NYSE:DBRG.PR.I), and the 7.125% Series J (NYSE:DBRG.PR.J). These like other non-investment grade fixed-income securities, have also seen their respective values fall drastically in response to the radically uncertain macro environment to offer yields in the range of 7.2% to 8%. For investors looking to maintain exposure to DBRG, they're the better choice. However, they could see their value fall further if consumer inflation ramps up from here and the Fed faces a recession while being forced to keep rates elevated.
